aliases: Hotdog with Egg and French Fries

english: Hotdog with Egg and French Fries

food_subgroup: Mixed snack plate with processed meat, egg, and fried potato

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn class exists for the combined hotdog-egg-fries plate. FOODON_00002473 (frankfurter) is used as the closest parent for the hotdog component. The composite dish is not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FMM (Sausages and similar products) is the closest defensible parent for the hotdog component of this composite dish. No composite plate code exists in FoodEx2 for this specific combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 covers the hotdog component only. The composite plate including egg and fries is not individually coded in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 2 hotdog sausages (80 g), 1 fried egg (50 g), and a portion of french fries (220 g), as typically served at a Filipino café.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Frankfurter (SR Legacy 07025), Fried egg (SR Legacy 01128), French fries deep-fried (SR Legacy 11352)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Filipino snack plate. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries: hotdog/frankfurter (approx. 23% weight), fried egg (approx. 14% weight), and deep-fried french fries (approx. 63% weight), reflecting a typical 350 g plate composition. Weights are proportional to the serving breakdown described in the serving section. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — French fries GI ~63 (deep-fried potato); egg GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate); frankfurter GI ~28 (low, processed meat). Composite GI estimated as carbohydrate-weighted average dominated by fries component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Filipino snack plate. GI is estimated as a carbohydrate-weighted average of components: deep-fried french fries (GI 63, dominant carbohydrate contributor ~88% of total carbohydrate), hotdog/frankfurter (GI 28, minor carbohydrate contributor ~8%), and fried egg (GI effectively 0, negligible carbohydrate ~4%). Weighted composite GI ≈ 63 × 0.88 + 28 × 0.08 + 0 × 0.04 ≈ 57.7, rounded to 58; however, given the dominance of the fries component and standard practice, GI is reported as 63 (fries-anchored estimate). GL per serving = (63 × 27.6 × 3.5) / 100 = 47.8 g. Source: Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI tables; University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from processed hotdog sausage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g, approximately 1085 mg per serving), which substantially contributes to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg and may elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from deep-fried potato fries
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the deep-fried potato fries contribute a high glycemic load (~47.8 GL per serving) and significant refined carbohydrate content, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction and substitution of fries with lower-GI vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from processed hotdog and deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its combination of saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g per 100 g), high sodium (~310 mg per 100 g), and cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g) from the processed hotdog sausage and deep-fried preparation, all of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotdog, Egg and Fries is a popular Filipino snack or light meal plate consisting of pan-fried or grilled hotdog sausages, a fried egg, and a generous portion of deep-fried potato french fries. It is commonly served at Filipino cafés and casual dining establishments in Singapore, such as Lucky Cafe on North Canal Road. The dish reflects the Filipino culinary tradition of combining Western-influenced processed meats with egg and fried starch as an affordable, filling snack or all-day breakfast option. The hotdog sausages are typically sweet-style Filipino-brand sausages (such as Purefoods or CDO), which are distinctly sweeter and more tender than Western frankfurters. The fries are deep-fried to a golden crisp, and the egg is usually fried sunny-side up or over-easy. The overall flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet from the hotdog, and rich from the fried egg and oil. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense (~243 kcal per 100 g), moderate in protein (~5.8 g per 100 g), and high in carbohydrates (~27.6 g per 100 g) dominated by the potato fries. Sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to the processed hotdog. The dish carries a high glycemic load per serving (~47.8) and is a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. In Singapore, this dish is typically priced around SGD 10.50 and is listed under Filipino snack offerings on food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: What is hotdog egg and fries?
Is hotdog egg and fries healthy?
Hotdog itlog at pritong patatas calories

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Filipino snack plate as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for the individual components (frankfurter, fried egg, deep-fried french fries). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 using a carbohydrate-weighted composite approach dominated by the fries component. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and based on approved sources, but exact restaurant-specific preparation variables (oil type, sausage brand, portion sizes) introduce uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for frankfurter, fried egg, and deep-fried french fries, weighted by approximate serving proportions (hotdog 23%, egg 14%, fries 63%). No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific composite dish. 2. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate anchored to deep-fried french fries (GI 63 per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. 3. The halal status is marked false because Filipino-style hotdog sausages typically contain pork or mixed meat and are not certified halal unless specifically stated; the restaurant Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road is not confirmed halal-certified. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not individually confirmed from a single approved source entry for this composite dish. 5. The description in the input JSON mentions beans ('Savor crispy fries, hotdog, egg, and beans!'); however, no beans component was confirmed from the field dataing or standard menu descriptions for this dish type. Beans have been excluded from the nutrient calculation pending confirmation; if beans are a standard component, the fibre, protein, and carbohydrate values should be revised upward. 6. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Filipino café plate portions and is not confirmed by the source restaurant.
